subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Thermodynamics tracks energy flow in reactions through enthalpy, entropy, and Gibbs free energy to determine if processes are spontaneous.
core_concepts: First law: energy is conserved; $\Delta U = q + w$; heat and work are forms of energy transfer.
Enthalpy ($H$) measures heat flow at constant pressure; $\Delta H < 0$ = exothermic; $\Delta H > 0$ = endothermic.
Hess's Law: $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$; enthalpy is a state function.
Calorimetry: $q = mc\Delta T$; for constant-volume bomb calorimeter, $q_{\text{rxn}} = -C_\text{cal}\Delta T$.
Entropy ($S$) measures disorder; second law: $\Delta S_{\text{univ}} > 0$ for spontaneous processes; gases > liquids > solids; more particles = more entropy.
Gibbs free energy: $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$; if $\Delta G < 0$, the process is spontaneous at that temperature.
The sign of $\Delta G$ depends on signs of $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ and the temperature; for $\Delta H > 0$ and $\Delta S > 0$, spontaneity occurs only at high $T$.

key_terms.definition: Heat content of a system at constant pressure; $\Delta H = q_p$
$\Delta H$ for a reaction equals the sum of $\Delta H$ values for any series of steps that produce the same net reaction
Enthalpy change when 1 mol of compound forms from elements in standard states; $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ for elements
Measure of disorder/randomness; increases with temperature, phase changes (s→l→g), and more particles
$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$; negative value means spontaneous process at constant $T$, $P$
Measurement of heat flow using $q = mc\Delta T$ at constant pressure (coffee cup) or constant volume (bomb)
Property that depends only on current state, not path taken (examples: $H$, $S$, $G$, $U$)

exam_tip: On FRQ Hess's Law problems, if you reverse a reaction, flip the sign of $\Delta H$; if you multiply by a coefficient, multiply $\Delta H$ by the same factor.
